title: Sensitivity of evolutionary entropy in Lefkovitch matrices
summary:
Evolutionary entropy, introduced by Demetrius, is a demographic invariant that quantifies the temporal organization of structured populations. Explicit sensitivity expressions for this quantity were derived by Demetrius, Gundlach and Ziehe for age-structured Leslie matrices, establishing the foundations of entropy-based perturbation theory.
  In this paper we develop a complete sensitivity theory for evolutionary entropy in irreducible Lefkovitch matrices. Using the Perron--Frobenius representation of the associated Markov chain, we derive explicit closed-form expressions for the stationary distribution, generation time, evolutionary entropy and its partial derivatives with respect to fertility, transition and retention parameters. The resulting identities are expressed directly in terms of demographic coefficients, Perron eigenvectors, the dominant eigenvalue and the reproductive potential.
  The entropy representation obtained here gives a natural decomposition into transition and retention components and clarifies the distinct mechanisms through which demographic uncertainty is generated in stage-structured populations. We further show that the theory specializes immediately to open-group Leslie matrices, a class that has been shown to comprise a large fraction of empirical demographic models.
  The results extend the entropy sensitivity theory of Demetrius--Gundlach--Ziehe from age-structured to general stage-structured populations and provide practical tools for comparative demographic analysis, perturbation studies, demographic robustness, and the investigation of life-history strategies. Several biological examples are presented, illustrating how entropy decomposition and sensitivity analysis reveal complementary aspects of population organization.

AI.tldr: This paper extends evolutionary entropy sensitivity theory from age-structured Leslie matrices to general stage-structured Lefkovitch matrices, deriving explicit closed-form expressions for entropy and its derivatives using Perron-Frobenius theory, with applications to comparative demography and life-history analysis.
AI.motivation: To generalize the existing entropy sensitivity theory (Demetrius-Gundlach-Ziehe) from age-structured to stage-structured populations, enabling entropy-based perturbation analysis for the broader class of Lefkovitch matrices that better represent real biological populations.
AI.method: Using Perron-Frobenius representation of the Markov chain associated with irreducible Lefkovitch matrices, deriving closed-form expressions for stationary distribution, generation time, evolutionary entropy, and partial derivatives with respect to fertility, transition, and retention parameters.
AI.result: Closed-form sensitivity expressions for evolutionary entropy in stage-structured populations; natural decomposition of entropy into transition and retention components; demonstration that the theory specializes to open-group Leslie matrices.
AI.conclusion: The work successfully extends entropy perturbation theory to general stage-structured populations, providing practical tools for comparative demographic analysis, robustness studies, and life-history strategy investigation, with biological examples illustrating the utility of entropy decomposition.

title: An Investigation of Additional Food Models with Generalised Functional Response
summary: Additional food sources are often used to improve the effectiveness of predators in controlling pest populations. However, the non-symmetric structure of additional food predator-prey models can cause certain aspects of their dynamics challenging to analyze. In this work, we study a general class of additional food models and establish conditions under which the coexistence equilibrium is globally stable. We then focus on a Holling type IV functional response with AF and show the existence of a Bogdanov-Takens bifurcation of codimension 3. We also study these models through the lens of deterministic chemical reaction network theory. Our analysis shows that the introduction of additional food increases the deficiency of the underlying reaction network and suggests a possible link between higher deficiency and complex bifurcations.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes additional food predator-prey models, proving global stability conditions for coexistence equilibrium and identifying a Bogdanov-Takens bifurcation of codimension 3 in Holling type IV models, while linking higher network deficiency to complex bifurcations via chemical reaction network theory.
AI.motivation: Additional food sources are commonly used to enhance predator effectiveness in pest control, but the non-symmetric structure of these models makes their dynamics challenging to analyze.
AI.method: The authors study a general class of additional food models, establish global stability conditions, analyze a Holling type IV functional response for Bogdanov-Takens bifurcation, and apply deterministic chemical reaction network theory.
AI.result: Global stability of coexistence equilibrium is established; a Bogdanov-Takens bifurcation of codimension 3 is found; additional food increases the deficiency of the underlying reaction network.
AI.conclusion: A possible link between higher network deficiency and complex bifurcations is suggested by the analysis.

title: Smoothly Time-Varying Continuous Time Markov Chains in Phylogenetics
summary: The dependence of evolutionary rate estimates on the timeframe of sampling poses a fundamental challenge for reconstructing evolutionary histories from molecular sequence data, which is central to evolutionary biology and infectious disease research. We present a novel and flexible approach to accommodate time-varying evolutionary rates by modeling the sequence substitution process using inhomogeneous continuous-time Markov chains (ICTMCs) acting along the branches of the phylogeny, and parameterizing the log transformed rate as a smooth function of time using a cubic B-spline basis expansion. Following the parlance of phylogenetics that refers to rates of molecular substitutions as molecular clocks, we call this a spline clock model. Integrals of the rate function over all branches, required for likelihood evaluation, are approximated efficiently using Gauss-Legendre quadrature, and smoothness is enforced by assigning a Gaussian Markov random field prior to the spline coefficients. Through a simulation study, we demonstrate that the spline clock model recovers the true time-varying rates more accurately and with tighter credible intervals than competing clock models. We apply the spline clock model to examine the evolutionary rate of foamy virus and the rate of spatial diffusion of SARS-CoV-2 across Europe, recovering strong time-varying signal in both settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a spline clock model using inhomogeneous continuous-time Markov chains with B-spline basis expansion to accommodate time-varying evolutionary rates, demonstrating better accuracy than competing models on simulated data and real applications to foamy virus and SARS-CoV-2.
AI.motivation: Evolutionary rate estimates depend on the timeframe of sampling, creating challenges for reconstructing evolutionary histories from molecular sequence data.
AI.method: Models sequence substitution using inhomogeneous continuous-time Markov chains (ICTMCs) with cubic B-spline basis expansion for log-transformed rates, efficiently approximated via Gauss-Legendre quadrature, with smoothness enforced by a Gaussian Markov random field prior.
AI.result: Spline clock model recovers true time-varying rates more accurately with tighter credible intervals than competing clock models in simulations; successfully applied to foamy virus evolution and SARS-CoV-2 spatial diffusion across Europe.
AI.conclusion: The spline clock model effectively captures time-varying evolutionary rates and outperforms existing approaches, with demonstrated utility in both pathogen evolutionary rate estimation and spatial diffusion analysis.

title: Closing the Loop to Discover Psychological Theories with an Automated Cognitive Scientist
summary: Across the sciences, autonomous systems are increasingly being used in closed-loop discovery, proposing new theories and designing and running experiments to test them. This approach is yet to be applied in the field of cognitive science, where the central bottleneck is theory-building: the creative step of turning the accumulated failures of existing models into better ones. Theory generation has remained manual even as data collection, modeling, and experiment design have been automated. We present the Automated Cognitive Scientist (AutoCog), a fully autonomous agentic-AI system that closes this loop. Large-language-model agents advocate competing theories, each expressed as an executable cognitive model, design experiments that best discriminate them, collect behavioral data from participants recruited online, score theories against collected data based on their generative performance, diagnose why they fail, and synthesize a better successor. Repeating this cycle allows them to search the space of theories, models, and experiments. In the domain of decision-making, AutoCog recovered known decision-making strategies from simulated behavior, including unconventional ones, showing that its discoveries are ultimately driven by the data rather than strictly bound by the priors of the underlying language models. When run with human participants, it produced theories that outperformed the established theories it was seeded with and generalized to held-out studies in two different experimental settings. It also surfaced a novel theory of multi-cue decision-making in which choices show diminishing sensitivity to feature values. The distinctive predictions of this theory were confirmed in a preregistered study with new participants. AutoCog demonstrates how an automated discovery system can be used to turn cognitive theory-building into an explicit, executable, and cumulative science.

AI.tldr: AutoCog is a fully autonomous AI system that closes the discovery loop in cognitive science by using LLM agents to generate, test, discriminate, and refine cognitive theories through iterative cycles of experimentation with human participants, successfully recovering known decision-making strategies and discovering a novel theory of multi-cue decision-making.
AI.motivation: While autonomous systems have been used for closed-loop discovery in other sciences, cognitive science still lacks automated theory-building—the creative step of turning accumulated experimental failures into better theories. Theory generation has remained manual even as data collection, modeling, and experiment design have been automated.
AI.method: AutoCog uses LLM agents to: (1) advocate competing theories expressed as executable cognitive models, (2) design experiments to discriminate between them, (3) collect behavioral data from online participants, (4) score theories based on generative performance, (5) diagnose why theories fail, and (6) synthesize better successor theories. This cycle iteratively searches the space of theories, models, and experiments.
AI.result: AutoCog recovered known and unconventional decision-making strategies from simulated behavior. With human participants, it produced theories outperforming established seed theories and generalized to held-out studies. It discovered a novel theory of multi-cue decision-making showing diminishing sensitivity to feature values, confirmed in a preregistered study.
AI.conclusion: AutoCog demonstrates that automated discovery can turn cognitive theory-building into an explicit, executable, and cumulative science, with discoveries driven by data rather than strictly bounded by LLM priors.

title: scBench-Long: Verifiable Benchmarking of Long-Horizon Single-Cell Biology
summary: Single-cell studies require analysts to convert raw measurements into specific biological claims through multi-step workflows and integration of metadata, assay context, and auxiliary evidence. Existing AI-biology benchmarks largely measure broad knowledge, executable workflows, or local analysis steps. We introduce scBench-Long, a benchmark for long-horizon single-cell biology in which agents must recover scientific conclusions from raw or near-raw data without prescribed methods. The benchmark contains 21 evaluations spanning melanoma CD8 T-cell reactivity, CD8 RNA+ATAC regulatory inference, human--monkey chimera development, KRAS-driven lung tumor aging, and lethal COVID-19 lung pathology. Tasks cover paired scRNA/TCR sequencing, RNA and chromatin profiling, cross-species transcriptomics, combinatorial scRNA-seq, single-nucleus RNA-seq, immune repertoires, ortholog maps, ligand--receptor resources, and validation evidence. Candidate claims are reproduced, reviewed, and converted into controlled answer vocabularies with deterministic grading and trajectory rubrics. Across 1,068 completed trajectories, the strongest model--harness pair passes 16/63 runs (25.4\%). scBench-Long evaluates whether agents can move beyond local analysis steps and make complex scientific claims that are supported by single-cell data.

AI.tldr: scBench-Long is a benchmark to test AI agents on long-horizon single-cell biology tasks, requiring them to derive scientific conclusions from raw data without prescribed methods. The strongest model achieves only 25.4% success across 1,068 trajectories.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-biology benchmarks focus on broad knowledge or isolated analysis steps, but fail to evaluate whether agents can perform multi-step reasoning to convert raw single-cell data into complex, biologically valid conclusions.
AI.method: The benchmark includes 21 evaluations spanning diverse single-cell modalities (scRNA-seq, TCR-seq, ATAC-seq, cross-species transcriptomics, etc.). Candidate scientific claims are reproduced, reviewed, and converted into controlled answer vocabularies with deterministic grading and trajectory rubrics.
AI.result: Across 1,068 completed trajectories, the best agent-harness combination successfully passes only 16 out of 63 runs (25.4% success rate).
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents struggle significantly with long-horizon single-cell biology reasoning, indicating a major gap between existing benchmarks and the complex, multi-step analytical workflows required in real single-cell research.

title: Budget-Constrained Compound Library Prioritization with Risk Awareness and Uncertainty Quantification
comment: 27 pages, 3 figures, 14 tables. Code and reproducibility artifacts available at https://github.com/ShengyaoLiang/risk-aware-compound-library-compression and archived at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20833014
summary: Early discovery projects often face a budgeted prioritization problem: many structures can be enumerated or purchased, but only a small fraction can be tested, reviewed, or synthesized first. I formulate this setting as risk-aware compound-library compression. Given a molecular library and a fixed Top-k budget, the goal is to return an enriched candidate subset while preserving uncertainty, applicability-domain evidence, ADMET/structural alerts, and audit fields needed for human review. The framework intentionally uses a transparent 2D activity proxy rather than a complex representation model, combining Morgan fingerprints, RDKit descriptors, a multilayer perceptron, split-conformal uncertainty intervals, leakage auditing, and auditable export. On ChEMBL 36, the model achieved Spearman 0.7674 and EF@1% 2.7331 on internal validation, and Spearman 0.5171 with EF@1% 2.4359 on a temporal holdout. After fold-0 training-overlap control, a scaffold-disjoint BACE subset retained ROC AUC 0.7626 and EF@1% 2.0253. In a strict 100-molecule BACE decision-layer replay, risk-aware ordering kept Hit@10 at 0.9000 while exposing review evidence that pure activity sorting omits. An EGFR/CHEMBL203 label-hidden operational replay supports workflow feasibility but is reported as same-source sensitivity analysis rather than independent external validation. The claim is bounded: the evidence supports risk-aware library compression as an upstream prioritization layer, while prospective blinded validation remains necessary before claiming project-specific hit-rate or cost improvements.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a risk-aware framework for compound library prioritization in early drug discovery, using transparent 2D descriptors and conformal prediction to return enriched subsets under budget constraints while preserving uncertainty and audit information.
AI.motivation: Early discovery projects face budgeted prioritization problems where many compounds exist but only a small fraction can be tested, reviewed, or synthesized first. The goal is to create a transparent, risk-aware method for selecting candidate subsets.
AI.method: The framework formulates compound library compression as a risk-aware Top-k selection problem, using Morgan fingerprints, RDKit descriptors, a multilayer perceptron, split-conformal uncertainty intervals, leakage auditing, and auditable export. It intentionally avoids complex representation models in favor of transparent 2D activity proxies.
AI.result: On ChEMBL 36: Spearman 0.7674, EF@1% 2.7331 (internal validation); Spearman 0.5171, EF@1% 2.4359 (temporal holdout). On BACE: ROC AUC 0.7626, EF@1% 2.0253 after fold-0 overlap control. In a 100-molecule BACE replay: Hit@10 0.9000 with enhanced review evidence.
AI.conclusion: The framework supports risk-aware library compression as an upstream prioritization layer, but prospective blinded validation is needed before claiming project-specific hit-rate or cost improvements.

title: pVACtools v6: A comprehensive suite for neoantigen prediction, visualization, and therapy design
summary:
With the rise of checkpoint blockade therapies and neoantigen-based vaccines reaching later-stage trials, there is a growing need for computational tools to identify and prioritize neoantigens. pVACtools, initially introduced in 2016, is an open-source informatic suite designed to support basic and translational neoantigen research. pVACtools assists prediction, prioritization, and visualization of neoantigens, as well as design of neoantigen-based therapies.
  We describe several major advances to pVACtools since the last update: (1) expanded neoantigen quality and safety assessment features, including support for peptide presentation scoring, immunogenicity prediction, anchor residue analysis, reference proteome similarity, percentile score calculation; (2) addition of pVACsplice, a new tool for predicting neoantigens from tumor-specific cis-splicing mutations; (3) addition of pVACbind, a flexible tool that supports noncanonical neoantigen sources; (4) improvement in neoantigen selection strategies; (5) a substantially improved pVACvector algorithm that achieves higher DNA/mRNA vector vaccine design success rates with shorter runtimes; (6) new utilities to support synthetic long peptide vaccine design; (7) extended prediction support for many non-human species; and (8) addition of pVACcompare, a tool to support comparison between two pVACseq results. Together, these updates reinforce pVACtools as the field's most comprehensive toolkit for neoantigen research, from basic discovery to the design and execution of personalized cancer vaccine clinical trials.

AI.tldr: pVACtools has been significantly updated with new features including pVACsplice for cis-splicing mutations, pVACbind for noncanonical neoantigens, pVACcompare for result comparison, improved vaccine design algorithms, and expanded safety/quality assessments, making it the most comprehensive neoantigen research toolkit.
AI.motivation: With checkpoint blockade therapies and neoantigen-based vaccines advancing to later-stage trials, there is a critical need for robust computational tools to identify and prioritize neoantigens for both basic research and clinical applications.
AI.method: pVACtools expands its suite with eight major updates: (1) enhanced quality/safety features (peptide presentation scoring, immunogenicity prediction, anchor residue analysis, proteome similarity, percentile scoring); (2) pVACsplice for cis-splicing mutation neoantigens; (3) pVACbind for noncanonical neoantigen sources; (4) improved selection strategies; (5) faster pVACvector with higher DNA/mRNA vaccine design success; (6) synthetic long peptide vaccine design utilities; (7) non-human species support; (8) pVACcompare for comparing two pVACseq results.
AI.result: The updates result in a substantially improved toolkit with higher vaccine design success rates, shorter runtimes, broader neoantigen source coverage (including cis-splicing and noncanonical), enhanced safety assessments, cross-species applicability, and comparative analysis capabilities.
AI.conclusion: These comprehensive updates reinforce pVACtools as the most complete open-source toolkit for neoantigen research, supporting the entire pipeline from basic discovery through personalized cancer vaccine clinical trial design and execution.

title: Semialgebraic Conditions for Identifying Triangles in Phylogenetic Networks
summary: An important consideration for a model-based method of phylogenetic network inference is the identifiability of the network parameter of the model. A recurring theme in previous works exploring this issue is that it is often difficult to identify the orientation of edges in a triangle of the network. In fact, it has been shown that for some models it is impossible to determine the orientation of triangle edges utilizing the standard algebraic technique of phylogenetic invariants. In this work, we consider one such model with a Jukes-Cantor site-substitution process and no coalescence. We give a complete semialgebraic description of three, 3-leaf Jukes-Cantor phylogenetic network models with embedded triangles. By describing these base cases, we resolve several questions about the identifiability of networks with embedded triangles. We show that for any pair of models, the intersection and set differences of the models are full-dimensional regions of the space of site-pattern probability distributions. Thus, despite being algebraically indistinguishable, these network models are not identical, nor are they identifiable (or generically identifiable). Our results also yield a straightforward biological interpretation--that the signal from a hybridization event may be immediately detectable but decays over time until it is impossible to identify the orientation of edges in the triangle of a network.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a complete semialgebraic description of three 3-leaf Jukes-Cantor phylogenetic network models with embedded triangles, showing that while these triangle orientations are algebraically indistinguishable via invariants, they are not identical—they occupy full-dimensional regions in probability space—and thus are not identifiable, with a biological interpretation that hybridization signal decays over time.
AI.motivation: To resolve the long-standing problem of determining whether different orientations of triangle edges in phylogenetic networks are identifiable under the Jukes-Cantor model with no coalescence, as previous work showed algebraic invariants cannot distinguish them.
AI.method: The authors provide a complete semialgebraic description (polynomial equations and inequalities) of three 3-leaf Jukes-Cantor phylogenetic network models with embedded triangles, and analyze the geometry of their intersection and set differences in the space of site-pattern probability distributions.
AI.result: For any pair of models, both the intersection and set differences are full-dimensional regions. This means the models are not identical, yet they are not identifiable (not even generically identifiable) because they overlap in full-dimensional sets.
AI.conclusion: The signal from a hybridization event is immediately detectable but decays over time, eventually making it impossible to identify the orientation of edges in a triangle of a network, despite the models being algebraically distinct in probability space.

title: On-farm management strategies for reducing H5N1 transmission in dairy cattle
summary: Introductions of H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b into dairy cattle have resulted in outbreaks on dairy farms across the United States since early-2024. Outbreaks have significant consequences for animal health, result in economic losses for the dairy industry, and pose a threat to human health. Though the relative contributions of different on-farm transmission pathways remain a key uncertainty, a major route is considered to be through repeated contamination of milking stalls (i.e. the equipment and area where an individual cow is milked) due to the milking of infected animals. Here we develop mathematical models of H5N1 transmission dynamics on dairy farms, considering multiple possible transmission pathways, and identify factors that contribute to outbreak risk and on-farm interventions for mitigating risk. In particular, we demonstrate that dividing cattle into 'milking cohorts', with cohorts kept in separate pens or paddocks and milked in the same order every day, would be highly effective at mitigating outbreaks irrespective of the dominant transmission pathway. Cohorting cattle is most effective when implemented pre-emptively (i.e. before an outbreak) and when newly introduced cattle are kept in the final milking cohort. Additionally, we demonstrate that frequent bulk milk sample testing (e.g. weekly) would enable the rapid detection of outbreaks and implementation of reactive interventions (or scaling up of existing interventions). Our findings can support the development of management guidelines for effectively responding to H5N1 outbreaks in dairy cattle.

AI.tldr: Mathematical models show that dividing dairy cattle into milking cohorts (same groups milked in consistent order) and frequent bulk milk testing are highly effective strategies for mitigating H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b outbreaks on dairy farms.
AI.motivation: H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b outbreaks in US dairy cattle since early 2024 cause significant animal health issues, economic losses, and human health threats, yet the contributions of different on-farm transmission pathways remain uncertain.
AI.method: Developed mathematical models of H5N1 transmission dynamics on dairy farms, considering multiple possible transmission pathways (including milking stall contamination), and evaluated intervention strategies such as milking cohorts and bulk milk sample testing.
AI.result: Milking cohorts are highly effective at mitigating outbreaks regardless of dominant transmission pathway, especially when implemented pre-emptively and when new cattle are placed in the final cohort. Frequent bulk milk testing (e.g. weekly) enables rapid outbreak detection and reactive interventions.
AI.conclusion: The findings can support development of management guidelines for effectively responding to H5N1 outbreaks in dairy cattle.

title: Surviving by Serving: Functional Relevance Drives Self-Organization in Complex Adaptive Systems
summary: Complex adaptive systems often develop organized structures without centralized control. Yet the local mechanisms by which functional organization emerges and persists remain incompletely understood. Here we propose Surviving by Serving (SBS) as a general principle of self-organization: components persist as long as their outputs are utilized by other components, whereas prolonged non-utilization promotes adaptation and exploration. To investigate this idea, we introduce a minimal multi-agent model in which agents transform shared resources and receive only local feedback when their outputs are subsequently utilized elsewhere in the system. Despite the absence of global objectives, the system spontaneously self-organizes into functional interaction networks. We observe the emergence of stable transformation chains, core-periphery organization, and the generation of novel states that enable previously inaccessible target conditions to be reached. Remarkably, self-sustaining interaction networks can arise even without external selection pressures, creating a pre-adaptive search phase from which later functional solutions emerge. These findings suggest that functional utilization may provide a simple, substrate-independent mechanism for the emergence and stabilization of organized structure in complex adaptive systems.

AI.tldr: A study proposing "Surviving by Serving" (SBS) as a self-organization principle where components persist based on how much their outputs are utilized by others, demonstrated through a minimal multi-agent model that spontaneously forms functional interaction networks without global objectives.
AI.motivation: Complex adaptive systems develop organized structures without centralized control, but the local mechanisms driving the emergence and persistence of functional organization remain poorly understood.
AI.method: A minimal multi-agent model where agents transform shared resources and receive only local feedback when their outputs are utilized elsewhere, testing whether functional organization emerges without global objectives or external selection pressures.
AI.result: Systems spontaneously self-organize into functional interaction networks, showing stable transformation chains, core-periphery organization, and novel states enabling previously inaccessible targets. Self-sustaining networks arise even without external selection, creating a pre-adaptive search phase.
AI.conclusion: Functional utilization may be a simple, substrate-independent mechanism for emergence and stabilization of organized structure in complex adaptive systems.

title: Hyperiax and Phylogenetic Inference from Shape Data
summary: Phylogenetic inference on high-dimensional morphological traits requires algorithms that account for both the nonlinear geometry of the shape data and the phylogenetic tree structure. The Backward Filtering Forward Guiding (BFFG) framework provides smoothing for nonlinear stochastic processes on trees and enables inference of parameters and ancestral states. As practical adoption has been limited by a lack of efficient implementations, we present Hyperiax, an open-source library for tree traversal algorithms and message passing using JAX, designed particularly to support operations needed for BFFG. Hyperiax enables efficient execution of operations on trees with large numbers of nodes and, coupled with the BFFG-specific operations, this allows efficient inference in both discrete-time and stochastic differential equation models. Concretely, we demonstrate that Hyperiax enables parameter inference and ancestral reconstruction for butterfly wing shapes represented by landmarks in two dimensions, and analyses of avian beaks from landmarks in three dimensions. Both cases demonstrate application of BFFG on two substantially larger phylogenetic trees with 850 and 696 nodes with higher resolution shape data (118 two-dimensional landmarks and 79 three-dimensional landmarks, specifically) than previously possible.

AI.tldr: Hyperiax is an open-source JAX-based library enabling efficient phylogenetic inference on high-dimensional morphological traits using the BFFG framework, demonstrated on butterfly wings (850 species, 118 landmarks) and avian beaks (696 species, 79 landmarks).
AI.motivation: Previous BFFG framework lacked efficient implementations for practical adoption, limiting its use for large phylogenetic trees and high-resolution shape data.
AI.method: Developed Hyperiax, an open-source library using JAX for tree traversal algorithms and message passing, supporting BFFG-specific operations for both discrete-time and stochastic differential equation models on trees.
AI.result: Demonstrated parameter inference and ancestral reconstruction for butterfly wing shapes (850 nodes, 118 2D landmarks) and avian beaks (696 nodes, 79 3D landmarks), substantially larger than previously possible.
AI.conclusion: Hyperiax enables practical large-scale phylogenetic inference on high-dimensional morphological traits, bridging the gap between the BFFG framework and real-world application.

title: Deep learning model emulators for marine biogeochemistry forecasting from days to decades
comment: 53 pages, 25 figures (incl. 10 in the Appendix), submitted to Biogeosciences
summary: Deep-learning emulators have emerged as a promising approach for reducing the computational cost of Earth System Models while potentially improving forecasting skill. Here, we demonstrate the successful emulation of a high-complexity marine biogeochemistry model within a simplified one-dimensional water-column framework. We explore two emulator architectures: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks that emulate a selected subset of variables at daily resolution, and physics-informed one-dimensional Convolutional Neural Networks (1D CNNs) that emulate the full pelagic system throughout the water column also at daily resolution. Using ocean physics simulator inputs, both emulators remain largely stable over multi-decadal timescales and accurately reproduce the parent model in both decadal climate projections and short-range (10-day) forecasting applications. The former includes the ability to predict the timing of phytoplankton Spring blooms several years in advance. When trained on reanalysis data, the emulators substantially outperform the parent model's forecast skill score for several key ecosystem variables, including phytoplankton and zooplankton. If similar performance can be achieved in three-dimensional regional applications, these emulators could provide substantially higher-quality predictions at a fraction of the computational cost. We further apply novel explainability techniques to identify key drivers of emulator behaviour and gain insights into emergent ecosystem dynamics. Performance is evaluated using a range of metrics, including the reproduction of daily variability and extreme events. These approaches have considerable potential for future applications in operational forecasting, climate-scale simulations, and marine autonomous systems.

AI.tldr: This paper successfully demonstrates that deep-learning emulators (LSTM and physics-informed 1D CNN) can accurately replicate a complex marine biogeochemistry model at much lower computational cost, remaining stable over decades and even outperforming the parent model in forecasting skill when trained on reanalysis data.
AI.motivation: To reduce the high computational cost of Earth System Models (specifically marine biogeochemistry components) while improving forecasting skill, by leveraging deep-learning emulators as a computationally cheaper alternative.
AI.method: Two emulator architectures were explored: (1) LSTM neural networks emulating a selected subset of variables at daily resolution, and (2) physics-informed 1D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) emulating the full pelagic system throughout the water column at daily resolution. Both were tested within a simplified one-dimensional water-column framework using ocean physics simulator inputs.
AI.result: Both emulators remained stable over multi-decadal timescales and accurately reproduced the parent model in decadal climate projections and 10-day forecasting. They predicted phytoplankton Spring bloom timing years in advance. When trained on reanalysis data, emulators substantially outperformed the parent model's forecast skill for key variables (phytoplankton, zooplankton). Novel explainability techniques identified key drivers of emulator behavior.
AI.conclusion: These deep-learning emulators show considerable potential for operational forecasting, climate-scale simulations, and marine autonomous systems, provided similar performance can be achieved in three-dimensional regional applications.

title: Implementation of reinforcement learning in chemical reaction networks: application to phototaxis as curiosity-driven exploration
comment: This paper is accepted as talk at ALIFE 2026 (Waterloo, Canada)
summary: Living systems navigate environments using noisy and incomplete sensory signals. In unicellular algae, phototaxis is often modeled as a mechanistic run--tumble process driven by stimulus--response rules. However, such descriptions overlook how organisms actively sample their environment to reduce sensory ambiguity. From a minimal cognition perspective, we reframe this navigation as a subjective, information-driven sensorimotor process. To this end, we propose a framework linking a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with biochemical reaction dynamics. Environmental variables are hidden, while the cell updates a minimal internal state from each observation through a memoryless Bayesian step. These internal dynamics balance orienting toward light with exploratory reorientation and can be implemented through Chemical-Reaction-Network Ordinary Differential Equations (CRN--ODEs). Our model includes a biophysical observation process for photoreception and a chemically computable polynomial bound on information gain. Using Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) on 30 experimentally recorded Chlamydomonas trajectories, we infer the behavioral objective consistent with observed phototactic motion and benchmark the resulting dynamics with standard Stochastic Simulation Algorithm (SSA) baselines. Our model reproduces the empirical alignment-to-light distribution, comparable to objective SSA baselines on this dataset. Within this framework, run--tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy: tumbling reorients the cell to sample new sensory configurations and resolve sensor ambiguity, demonstrating how intracellular biochemical networks can support adaptive information-seeking behavior in cellular navigation.

AI.tldr: This paper reframes phototaxis in unicellular algae as an information-driven sensorimotor process using a POMDP framework with CRN-ODEs and Inverse Reinforcement Learning, showing that run-tumble behavior emerges as an information-acquisition strategy to resolve sensory ambiguity.
AI.motivation: To move beyond mechanistic stimulus-response models of phototaxis and reframe navigation as a subjective, information-driven process where organisms actively sample their environment to reduce sensory ambiguity, viewed through a minimal cognition perspective.
AI.method: The authors propose a POMDP framework linked with biochemical reaction dynamics, using memoryless Bayesian updates on a minimal internal state, implemented via CRN-ODEs. They include a biophysical observation process and a polynomial bound on information gain. Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) is applied to 30 Chlamydomonas trajectories to infer behavioral objectives, benchmarked against SSA baselines.
AI.result: The model reproduces empirical alignment-to-light distributions comparably to objective SSA baselines on the dataset. Run-tumble alternation emerges as an information-acquisition strategy where tumbling reorients the cell to sample new sensory configurations and resolve ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: Run-tumble behavior is not merely a mechanistic response but an adaptive information-seeking strategy; intracellular biochemical networks can support such cognitive-like navigation, demonstrating how minimal internal states can drive adaptive behavior in cellular systems.

title: KG-TRACE: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Mechanistic Grounding in Antimicrobial Resistance Prediction
summary:
While WGS-based AMR prediction has reached high accuracy, existing models lack a mechanism to ground neural attributions in established biological pathways. We present KG-TRACE, a novel neuro-symbolic framework that integrates the WHO mutation knowledge graph (KG) as a structured biological constraint on a neural genomic model. Unlike existing methods that learn statistical patterns in isolation, KG-TRACE fuses genomic features and RotatE-based KG embeddings through a learned epistemic trust gate, dynamically weighting neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge.
  Evaluated on the CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort, KG-TRACE achieves an AUROC of 0.9760 for isoniazid, achieving competitive accuracy while its primary value lies in symbolic grounding, not predictive uplift. More importantly, we introduce the Biological Grounding Ratio (BGR), a dataset-level metric that quantifies alignment between neural attributions and established biology. Our framework achieves a 92.5% symbolic coverage of isoniazid-resistant predictions and effectively identifies MDR co-occurrence artifacts by issuing laboratory follow-up flags for 'UNCERTAIN' cases. We demonstrate that neuro-symbolic grounding provides a verifiable audit trail for clinicians, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and clinical trust.

AI.tldr: KG-TRACE is a neuro-symbolic framework integrating WHO mutation knowledge graphs with neural genomic models for AMR prediction, achieving 0.9760 AUROC on M. tuberculosis with 92.5% symbolic coverage and introducing the Biological Grounding Ratio metric for auditability.
AI.motivation: Existing WGS-based AMR prediction models achieve high accuracy but lack grounding in established biological pathways, making neural attributions unverifiable and limiting clinical trust.
AI.method: KG-TRACE integrates RotatE-based KG embeddings with genomic features via a learned epistemic trust gate, dynamically weighting neural evidence against symbolic biological knowledge from the WHO mutation KG.
AI.result: Achieves 0.9760 AUROC for isoniazid on CRyPTIC M. tuberculosis cohort, 92.5% symbolic coverage of resistant predictions, and identifies MDR co-occurrence artifacts with laboratory follow-up flags.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-symbolic grounding provides a verifiable audit trail, bridging predictive accuracy and clinical trust through the proposed Biological Grounding Ratio metric.

title: Beyond Single-Source Cognitive Taskonomy:Multi-Source Task Relations through fMRI Transfer Learning
summary: Cognitive tasks are organized by shared and specialized neural processes. Masked fMRI reconstruction provides a common self-supervised objective for quantifying transfer relations among task states, but existing reconstruction-based taskonomies mainly study one-to-one transfer from a single source task to a target. Here, we extend an fMRI cognitive taskonomy from single-source to multi-source transfer across 23 Human Connectome Project task states and use Boolean Integer Programming (BIP) to analyze budget-constrained task allocation. We train 1,127 task-specific and transfer models. Single-source transfer is directional and paradigm structured: motor states transfer well within the motor paradigm but provide limited support to most non-motor targets, consistent with a shared sensorimotor execution system and effector-specific representations. Multi-source transfer depends on the composition of the source set, suggesting that many-to-one task relations are not fully captured by pairwise taskonomy alone. Across supervision budgets, BIP repeatedly allocates direct supervision to several 0-back and 2-back working-memory states, although these states are not consistently the strongest individual sources. This pattern may reflect the integration of perceptual, attentional, and executive processes in working-memory tasks. Together, these findings reveal a cross-paradigm-limited motor cluster and working-memory states with high priority under the specified global allocation objective. Our study extends reconstruction-based fMRI taskonomy from one-to-one transfer to many-to-one task relations and budget-constrained task dependencies.

AI.tldr: This paper extends fMRI-based cognitive taskonomy from single-source to multi-source transfer across 23 HCP task states, using Boolean Integer Programming to analyze budget-constrained task allocation, revealing a cross-paradigm-limited motor cluster and high-priority working-memory states.
AI.motivation: To move beyond one-to-one transfer relationships in fMRI task analysis and study many-to-one task relations under budget constraints, enabling more comprehensive understanding of how cognitive tasks share and specialize neural processes.
AI.method: Used Masked fMRI reconstruction as self-supervised objective, trained 1,127 task-specific and transfer models across 23 HCP task states, applied Boolean Integer Programming (BIP) for budget-constrained task allocation analysis.
AI.result: Single-source transfer is directional and paradigm-structured (motor states transfer well within motor paradigm but poorly to non-motor targets); multi-source transfer depends on source composition; BIP consistently allocates direct supervision to working-memory states (0-back and 2-back) across budgets.
AI.conclusion: Extends fMRI taskonomy from one-to-one to many-to-one relations; reveals motor cluster with cross-paradigm limitations and working-memory states as high-priority under global allocation objectives.

title: Frustrated shapes of solid domains in fluid membrane vesicles: From rolls and folds to crumples and wrinkles
summary: Fluid-solid composite vesicles, comprising 2D solid domains integrated into a topologically-closed fluid bilayer membrane, exhibit complex morphologies arising from the geometric frustration between spherical closure of the membrane and 2D solid elasticity. This scenario is distinct from the better studied case of multi-fluid domain vesicles. Here, we study the elastic energies and shape equilibria of a closed vesicle membrane containing a single, flexible circular solid domain using discrete finite-element (Surface Evolver) simulations, determining the key physical and mechanical parameters to govern shape selection. While we find that the 2D solid (shear) elasticity has minimal impact on the highly-under inflated morphologies, the geometrically non-linear resistance of the solid to Gaussian curvature substantially impacts the shape and elastic patterns form for inflated vesicles, by an amount that it grows with ratio of vesicle size to the elastic thickness of solid. For sufficiently large (thin) vesicles we characterize a generic sequence of ground state patterns of solid shape with increasing inflation: from cylindrical rolls and isometric folds to spatially complex patterns of crumples and wrinkles and ultimately to smooth caps. This sequence of non-isometric patterns at high-inflation is shown to be governed by the same far-from-threshold mechanics used to describe similar shape transitions in microscopic sheets on curved liquid interfaces, establishing that inflated shapes are governed by two basic mechanical scales of membrane tension. We find our predictions for highly-anisotropic shape equilibria of fluid-solid composite vesicles closely match experimentally observed shapes of giant unilamellar vesicles of phase-separated DPPC and DOPC.

AI.tldr: This paper studies fluid-solid composite vesicles (2D solid domains in fluid bilayer membranes) using finite-element simulations. It finds that solid shear elasticity minimally impacts under-inflated shapes, but Gaussian curvature resistance strongly affects inflated morphologies. A sequence of ground state patterns emerges with inflation: rolls→folds→crumples/wrinkles→smooth caps. Predictions match experimental DPPC/DOPC vesicle shapes.
AI.motivation: To understand the geometric frustration between spherical membrane closure and 2D solid elasticity in fluid-solid composite vesicles, which is distinct from the better-studied multi-fluid domain vesicles.
AI.method: Discrete finite-element (Surface Evolver) simulations of a closed vesicle membrane containing a single flexible circular solid domain, analyzing key physical and mechanical parameters governing shape selection.
AI.result: Shear elasticity has minimal impact on under-inflated morphologies; Gaussian curvature resistance substantially impacts inflated vesicle shapes. A generic sequence of ground state patterns emerges with increasing inflation: cylindrical rolls → isometric folds → crumples/wrinkles → smooth caps. High-inflation shapes are governed by membrane tension mechanics.
AI.conclusion: The sequence of non-isometric patterns at high inflation follows the same mechanics as shape transitions in microscopic sheets on curved liquid interfaces. Predictions closely match experimentally observed shapes of phase-separated DPPC/DOPC giant unilamellar vesicles.

title: GRAINS: Storage-Aware Algorithm-Architecture Co-Design Enabling High-Performance and Low-Cost Graph-Based Genome Analysis
summary:
Graph-based representations of genome sequences have emerged as a powerful approach for representing massive genomic databases in an expressive and efficient way. Despite their benefits, analysis on large-scale genome graphs incurs significant data movement overhead from the storage system due to accessing large amounts of low-reuse data. Processing data directly inside the storage device can be a fundamental solution for mitigating this overhead. However, none of the existing tools for graph-based genome analysis can be efficiently used inside the storage system due to the limited internal hardware resources in modern SSDs. At the same time, prior storage-centric systems developed for (i) traditional, linear non-graph-based genome analysis or (ii) conventional, non-genomic graph analysis are not suitable for the unique data structures and access patterns of graph-based genome analysis.
  We propose GRAINS, the first system for analysis with large-scale genome graphs in storage. Through our detailed examination of typical analysis pipelines that operate on genome graphs, we perform storage-aware algorithm-architecture co-design to (i) make these pipelines more storage-friendly and (ii) further improve performance, energy-efficiency, and cost via in-storage and in-flash processing. GRAINS's co-design is based on three key aspects. First, we propose a new batching and execution flow, based on unique features of genome graphs. Second, via in-flash and in-storage processing, we avoid transferring low-reused flash pages. Third, to leverage the full parallelism of flash dies, we design an effective, yet lightweight, scheduling technique, enabled by re-purposing the existing SSD structures. GRAINS provides 2.7x-47.8x speedup (4.4x-31.6x energy reduction) over the state-of-the-art software baselines, and 1.5x-17.0x speedup (3.1x-20.7x energy reduction) over a hardware-accelerated baseline.

AI.tldr: GRAINS is the first system for genome graph analysis inside SSDs, using algorithm-architecture co-design (batching, in-flash/in-storage processing, lightweight scheduling) to achieve 2.7x-47.8x speedup and 4.4x-31.6x energy reduction over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: Graph-based genome analysis incurs significant data movement overhead from storage due to large, low-reuse data access. Existing tools cannot run inside SSDs (limited hardware resources), and prior storage-centric systems for linear genome analysis or non-genomic graph analysis are unsuitable for genome graph data structures and access patterns.
AI.method: The authors propose GRAINS based on three co-design aspects: (1) a new batching and execution flow leveraging unique genome graph features, (2) in-flash and in-storage processing to avoid transferring low-reused flash pages, and (3) a lightweight scheduling technique that re-purposes existing SSD structures to exploit flash die parallelism.
AI.result: GRAINS achieves 2.7x-47.8x speedup and 4.4x-31.6x energy reduction over state-of-the-art software baselines, and 1.5x-17.0x speedup with 3.1x-20.7x energy reduction over a hardware-accelerated baseline.
AI.conclusion: In-storage processing with algorithm-architecture co-design is an effective approach for graph-based genome analysis, dramatically improving performance and energy efficiency while overcoming SSD hardware constraints.

title: Shape-Constrained Bayesian Active Learning of Self-Limiting Saturation Curves
summary: Self-limiting saturation curves, monotone responses that rise from zero to a plateau, govern gas adsorption, enzyme kinetics, dose-response pharmacology, and the growth per cycle of atomic layer deposition (ALD), yet mapping each curve from a handful of costly measurements is a shared bottleneck. The standard surrogate, a stationary-kernel Gaussian process, enforces no shape constraint: on sparse, noisy data it produces unphysical dips that corrupt both the inferred curve and the uncertainty used to choose the next experiment. We present an active-learning platform built on Bayesian monotonic I-spline regression, in which every posterior curve rises from exactly zero and never decreases, the plateau is set by a measurement at maximum exposure rather than a prior, and the input at any saturation level is read from the posterior by level crossing with no kinetic model assumed. Benchmarked isotherm by isotherm on five kinetically distinct families (Langmuir, dissociative Michaelis-Menten, sigmoidal Sips, logarithmic Elovich, and dispersive Kohlrausch-Williams-Watts), with ALD process development as the working example, the same fixed surrogate recovers every curve to within measurement noise without a single non-monotone posterior draw, and noise-free sweeps show the basis itself is near-exact across each family's regimes, locating its single capacity boundary at the sharpest sigmoidal onset. Driven by ordinary uncertainty sampling, the platform reaches noise-floor accuracy within a 20-measurement budget in every regime, in as few as seven measurements, whereas random sampling succeeds in only two of the five; the predicted pulse times err only on the conservative side, toward longer pulses, near saturation. Because the basis privileges no kinetic form, the platform applies wherever a self-limiting response must be learned from scarce data.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an active-learning platform using Bayesian monotonic I-spline regression to model self-limiting saturation curves (like ALD, enzyme kinetics, gas adsorption) without assuming any specific kinetic model. It reliably recovers curves from sparse, noisy data, requires as few as 7-20 measurements, and outperforms standard Gaussian processes by enforcing monotonicity and avoiding unphysical dips.
AI.motivation: Self-limiting saturation curves appear across many scientific fields, but mapping them from sparse, costly measurements is a shared bottleneck. Standard Gaussian process surrogates produce unphysical non-monotonic dips on noisy data, corrupting both the inferred curve and experimental decision-making.
AI.method: Bayesian monotonic I-spline regression with active learning via uncertainty sampling. The approach enforces that posterior curves rise monotonically from exactly zero, sets the plateau using a measurement at maximum exposure, and reads saturation levels by level crossing without assuming any kinetic model.
AI.result: The fixed surrogate recovers every curve across five kinetically distinct families to within measurement noise with zero non-monotonic posterior draws. Active learning reaches noise-floor accuracy within 7-20 measurements in all regimes, while random sampling succeeds in only two of five families. Predicted pulse times err only conservatively (toward longer pulses) near saturation.
AI.conclusion: The platform applies wherever a self-limiting response must be learned from scarce data because it privileges no specific kinetic form, making it broadly useful across gas adsorption, enzyme kinetics, pharmacology, and ALD process development.

title: State-Specific Respiratory Signatures for Affective and Stress Recognition: Interpretable Respiratory Markers, Autocorrelation Lags, and Compact CNN Models
summary: Respiratory activity is a direct and interpretable physiological channel for wearable stress and affective-state recognition, yet many studies emphasize classification accuracy without identifying which respiratory properties separate different states. This work reframes RESP-based recognition as a joint predictive and explanatory problem. Using the chest respiratory channel of the WESAD dataset, we analyze 60 s windows under leave-one-subject-out validation and combine two complementary branches: compact raw-signal one-dimensional convolutional neural networks (1D-CNNs) and physically grouped handcrafted respiratory signatures. The primary application task is binary stress versus non-stress detection, while baseline, stress, amusement, and meditation are additionally analyzed in a one-vs-rest setting to reveal state-specific respiratory markers. The feature space is organized into respiratory timing, breath-to-breath variability, waveform statistics, spectral/time-frequency descriptors, and autocorrelation/nonlinear predictability descriptors, with the raw 60 s signal treated as a sixth representation for the CNN branch. We introduce autocorrelation transition lags (Zpm/Zmp) as interpretable markers of respiratory correlation scale and separately evaluate exploratory FEG-Pro/Lyapunov-like descriptors. In the final CNN refit setting, the raw-signal model achieved the strongest stress-vs-rest performance, with accuracy 96.72 percent, macro-F1 95.30 percent, and MCC 90.61 percent. In contrast, compact feature models were stronger for baseline, with MCC 65.34 percent, amusement, with MCC 35.69 percent, and especially meditation, with MCC 88.65 percent. These results show that CNNs are most useful for the practical stress detector, whereas interpretable respiratory signatures provide stronger and more physiologically transparent state-specific markers for several non-stress conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper reframes respiratory-based stress/affect recognition as both a predictive and explanatory problem, combining 1D-CNNs with handcrafted respiratory features. Using the WESAD dataset, CNNs achieve 96.72% accuracy for stress detection, while interpretable respiratory signatures (especially for meditation, MCC 88.65%) provide better state-specific markers.
AI.motivation: Many existing respiratory-based stress studies focus only on classification accuracy without explaining which respiratory properties differentiate emotional/physiological states. This work aims to jointly predict and explain.
AI.method: Two complementary branches: (1) compact 1D-CNNs on raw 60s chest respiratory signals, (2) handcrafted features organized into timing, variability, waveform, spectral/time-frequency, autocorrelation/nonlinear predictability descriptors. Evaluated on WESAD dataset with leave-one-subject-out validation for binary stress vs. non-stress and one-vs-rest settings.
AI.result: CNN model achieved best stress-vs-rest performance (accuracy 96.72%, macro-F1 95.30%, MCC 90.61%). Handcrafted feature models were stronger for baseline (MCC 65.34%), amusement (MCC 35.69%), and meditation (MCC 88.65%).
AI.conclusion: CNNs are most effective for practical binary stress detection, while interpretable respiratory signatures provide stronger, more physiologically transparent markers for non-stress conditions, especially meditation.

title: The parental parsimony problem on binary, tree-child phylogenetic networks
summary: Phylogenetic reconstruction is one of the major challenges in computational biology. Among existing reconstruction methods for phylogenetic networks, an important subtask emerges in extending a leaf-labelling on a phylogenetic network to determine a most parsimonious tree inside the network. There exist different variants of this subtask depending on the biological model assumptions for which distinct evolutionary phenomena are captured by the network. In this article we assume that next to hybridization or recombination events, also allopolyploidy or incomplete lineage sorting are present. Then, finding the most parsimonious tree inside the network is called the parental parsimony score problem (PPS), a NP-hard combinatorial optimization problem. We provide the first constant-factor approximation for the PPS on arbitrary but fixed leaf labels and a class of networks on which the PPS remains NP-hard, namely binary, semi-simplex, tree-child phylogenetic networks. Furthermore, we introduce a novel exact solution algorithm for the PPS on binary, tree-child phylogenetic networks and analyze its performance on simulated data.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first constant-factor approximation algorithm for the NP-hard Parental Parsimony Score (PPS) problem on binary, semi-simplex, tree-child phylogenetic networks, along with a novel exact solution algorithm tested on simulated data.
AI.motivation: Phylogenetic reconstruction is a major challenge in computational biology. The subtask of finding the most parsimonious tree inside a phylogenetic network becomes computationally hard when accounting for multiple evolutionary phenomena (hybridization, allopolyploidy, incomplete lineage sorting), formalized as the NP-hard PPS problem.
AI.method: The authors provide (1) the first constant-factor approximation algorithm for PPS on arbitrary but fixed leaf labels on binary, semi-simplex, tree-child phylogenetic networks, and (2) a novel exact solution algorithm for PPS on binary, tree-child phylogenetic networks.
AI.result: A constant-factor approximation is achieved for NP-hard PPS on a specific class of networks. The exact algorithm's performance is analyzed on simulated data.
AI.conclusion: This work advances the state-of-the-art by providing the first approximation guarantee for PPS and a new exact method, addressing a computationally challenging problem in phylogenetic network analysis.
